claims.claim: A European bank transitioned intelligent PDU vendors while maintaining a single unified control platform across legacy and new hardware
claims.sourceTitle: Data Centre PDU Migration & Rack Security | European Bank Case Study
claims.excerpt: The solution had to support both legacy and new PDUs simultaneously, while keeping power monitoring, environmental data, and security workflows under one roof.

claims.claim: PDU control software notes there is still no single simple way to communicate with all PDUs across brands despite SNMP support
claims.excerpt: Though many support SNMP, there's still no single simple way to communicate with all PDUs if you have a mix of brands.

claims.claim: Standardized protocols solve device communication but not the data-mapping problem when devices vary by model or firmware
claims.sourceTitle: DCIM Device Templates: Adapt Any Device Without Coding - Modius, Inc.
claims.excerpt: Standard protocols like Modbus, SNMP, and BACnet solve the communication problem between DCIM and devices — but they do not solve the data mapping problem.

claims.claim: Protocol diversity across SNMP, Modbus, and BACnet creates operational complexity and fragmented visibility requiring DCIM normalization
claims.sourceTitle: Protocol Diversity in Data Centers: DCIM Solutions - Modius, Inc.
claims.excerpt: Protocol diversity—the use of multiple device communication standards like SNMP, Modbus, and BACnet across data center infrastructure—creates operational complexity, fragmented visibility, and increased risk.

tensions.text: Mordor rates intelligent-PDU market concentration as only 'Medium,' which undercuts a foreclosure thesis (Legrand's Raritan+Server Technology dual-brand lock) that would justify treating serviceable demand as regulatorily gated and haircut to 0.45–0.65 primarily via EU certification barriers rather than competitive structure.

Mordor Intelligence's own report gives two conflicting CAGR figures for the same 2025-2030 intelligent-PDU forecast period (3.52% in the summary table vs. 9.44% in the narrative text), undermining reliability of using this source to quantify serviceable/addressable growth.

Evidence of PDU vendor switching (bank case study achieving unified control across legacy/new vendors) is in tension with evidence that mixed-brand PDU communication still lacks a single simple standard and that protocol standardization does not solve data-mapping/integration friction — meaning the degree of vendor lock (relevant to the Legrand dual-brand foreclosure concern underlying serviceableFactor) is empirically ambiguous, not settled either way.

absences: No Omdia or Mordor vendor-share table was found showing Legrand-owned Raritan + Server Technology's combined share of the intelligent-PDU segment specifically; Omdia's Rack PDU Market Tracker/Annual Analysis pages return only paywalled abstracts with vendor shares locked behind subscription.
No numeric top-3 (or top-5) concentration percentage for the intelligent-PDU segment was found; Mordor only supplies a qualitative 'Medium' concentration label with no percentage breakdown.

No quantified figure for Legrand's own combined (Raritan+Server Technology) rack-PDU or intelligent-PDU market share was found in any source — only a qualitative claim that the acquisitions 'consolidated the #1/#2 rack-PDU positions in North America.'

No quantified cost or time estimate for switching intelligent-PDU vendors (re-integration effort into the monitoring/DCIM layer) was found; only qualitative case-study and protocol-interoperability narratives exist, with no benchmark figure to support or refute the serviceableFactor's regulatory-based (vs. competitive-lock-based) haircut rationale.
